text: COTAGAITA, or Santiago-de-Cotagayta, a town of Bolivia, in the intendancy and 90 m. SSE of Potosi, prov. of Chichas, on the Cotagaita, 30 m. W of the junction of that river with the Pilcomayo. The C. descends from the Andes, and has a total course in an easterly direction of about 180 m.
COTAPAXI. See COTOPAXI.
COTAPORT, or COOTAPORT, a village of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, 14 m. ESE of Tellicherry, and 9 m. E of Mahi.
COTAPURAMBA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, 6 m. NE of Tellicherry, on the N side of a ramification of the Western Ghauts.
COTARINGAN, a river of Borneo, which flows SW and falls into the Java sea.
COTASIR, a town of India, in the prov. of Cutch, on the estuary of the Koree, the largest deltoid branch of the Ganges, which is here about 7 m. wide.

COTAYBETTA, an immense isolated mountain of India, in the district of Kurg, 9 m. N of Muddukayray. Its base occupies a large extent of country; its sides are clothed with forest, and innumerable cultivated valleys occupy the recesses in them; its summit forms a kind of undulating table-land. On its highest point is a fine reservoir of water, which retains a constant supply in all seasons, and close to it is a rude temple.

COTEAU-DES-PRAIRIES, an extensive ridge or swell in the prairies of N America, which, commencing in about 46° N lat., and between 98° and 99° W long., runs SSE between the tributaries of the St. Peter river and those of the Missouri, and extends S as far as the head of the Blue-Earth river, where it gradually widens and sinks to the level of the surrounding country. It is formed by the elevation of granitic or other primitive rocks; and may be consider- | COT ed as the dividing range between the Mississippi and the Missouri basins in that quarter. Its entire length is about 200 m. Its N termination, called Tete-du- Coteau, is not more than from 15 to 20 m. across; and has an alt. above Big-Stone lake of 890 ft., and above the ocean of 1,916 ft. Under the 44th parallel, its breadth is 40 m., and its mean elevation 1,450 ft.; and here its slopes are rather abrupt, covered with verdure, and scolloped by deep ravines thickly shaded with bushes.
COTE-AUX-FE'ES (LA), a village of Switzerland, in the canton and 21 m. WSW of Neufchatel. In the environs, in the Jura range, occur several celebrated grottoes and caverns.

COTE-BLANCHE, a bay on the coast of Louisiana, U.S., in the co. of Attakapas, between Atchafalaya bay on the NW, and Vermilion bay on the E. It is 18 m. in length from NE to SW, and 21 m. in breadth from NW to SE.
COTECKNEY, a river in the state of North Carolina, U. S., which rises in Franklin co., and running from NW to SE, joins the Neuse, on the l. bank, after a course of 75 m.

COTE'-D'OR, a department of France, forming part of the ancient province of Bourgogne, and deriving its name from a mountain-range SW of Dijon. It is situated between the parallels of 46° 55' and 48° 21' N; and is bounded on the N by Aube; on the NE by Haute-Marne; E by Haute-Seine and Jura; S by Saone and Loire; W and NW by Nievre and Yonne. Its greatest length from N to S is 84 m.; breadth from E to W 75 m. Its area is 880,359 hectares; or about 3,355 sq. m. The department is mountainous and traversed by several chains of hills; those of Coté-d'Or stretching SW from the Cevennes to the source of the Dheune on the plateau of Langres. Among the rivers which have their source in this district, are, on the NW, the Seine, and its affluents the Armançon, the Aube, the Ource, and the Serain; on the E the Saone, and its affluents the Tille, the Ouche, the Dheune, and the Vingeane; and on the SW, the Arroux, an affluent of the Loire. In addition to these facilities for navigation, the dep. has the advantage of the Bourgogne, and the Rhone and Rhine canals.—The climate is temperate. The air keen, pure, and wholesome. The soil is much varied, but more or less stony throughout, and especially around the mountains. To the E and S it is rich and fertile. In the environs of the Saone, it is mixed with a considerable quantity of sand and flint, and is unproductive. The natural division of the soil is 212,121 hectares heaths and wastes; 98,000 h. rich land; 130,191 h. calcareous soil; and 576,254 h. stony land. - The agricultural products are corn, maize, rye, barley, and oats, beyond the average consumption. The best wheat and oats are produced in the celebrated valleys of Montbart, Epoisses, and St. Thibaut. Hemp, flax, and various oleaginous plants are also cultivated here. The soil appropriate to the growth of the vine in this dep. is computed at 26,177 hectares. The wines are in high repute; and a great quantity is exported. The first growth comprehends Clos-Vougeot, Chambertin, La Romanie, Saint-Vivant, Richebourg, St. George's, La Tâche, and Nuits. Of the second class are those of Beaune, Pomard, Volnay, La Perriere, Montrachet, Meursault, and the Goutte-d'Or. The annual produce of the vine is estimated at 520,550 hectolitres. The superficial area of forest-land may be about 228,869 hectares, chiefly oak, beach, and elm. The chestnut is not found here. The horses and horned cattle are numerous; and a considerable number of merino sheep and pigs are reared. The ass is employed in agriculture in the inferior districts of this dep. The honey-bee is reared, and game is abundant. The rivers are well stocked with fish, trout in particular. The mountainous districts yield iron, coal, marble, mill-stones, and free-stone. Iron forms an important branch of trade; there are a considerable number of forges, and the manufacture of hardware gives employment to a great many. There are also manufactories of cotton, woollen, linen, paper, and vinegar, tanneries, and dyeing and chemical works. The principal exports are wine, grain, wood, and iron-wares, and some trade in leather is also carried on. The down of the geese reared on the borders of the Tille, the Ouche, and the Saone, forms an important branch of trade with St. Jean-de-Losne, celebrated for its cheeses. The facilities for transit, either by land or water, are very great, and contribute powerfully to the commercial prosperity of this dep. — The pop. in 1801 was 340,500; in 1831, 375,063; in 1841, 393,316; in 1846, 396,524. It is divided into 4 arrondissements, viz. Beaune, Chatillon, Dijon, and Semur; which are subdivided into 33 cantons, and 726 communes. Under the former regime, it sent 5 members to the chamber of deputies, who were elected in 1834 by 2,464 electors. It is under the jurisdiction of the cour royale of Dijon. The dep. constitutes the diocese of Dijon, whose bishop is suffragan to the archbishop of Lyon. In 1842 it possessed 18 secondary establishments for education, attended in all by 914 pupils; 1 of these is the royal college of Dijon, and 7 are communal colleges. In 1840 it possessed 1 normal school, 19 superior schools, with 720 pupils; and 889 elementary schools, attended by 54,972 scholars in winter, and 30,008 in summer.

COTENTIN, a district of France, in the dep. of La Manche, forming a peninsular projection from the mainland, and surrounded on the W, N, and E, by the English channel. It is about 80 m. in length from NNW to SSE, and 30 m. in breadth. Besides the cap., Coutance, the towns of Granville, Saint-Lo, Carentan, Valogne, and Cherbourg, are in this district.

COTE-SAINT-ANDRE' (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, arrond. of Vienne.—The cant., comprising 13 com., had a pop. of 13,930 in 1841.—The town, 17 m. ESE of Vienne, had a pop. of 4,083 in 1841. Excellent white wines, known as Eaux-de-la-Côte, are made in the environs.
COTE-SANS-DESSEIN, a village in Calaway co., in the state of Missouri, U. S., 11 m. E by N of Jefferson, on the N bank of the Missouri. Pop. 609.
COTES, a township in the p. of Eccleshall, in Staffordshire, 7 m. NW by W of Stafford. Area 820 acres. Pop. 328.
COTES-D'ARCY (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Vienne. Pop. 1,129.

COTES - DU - NORD, a maritime province of France; bounded by the English channel on the N; on the E by the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine; on the S by Morbihan; and on the W by Finistère. It lies between the parallels of 48° 3' and 48° 57' N; and comprises an area of 691,085 hectares. A chain of granite hills, of which the culminating points are Mont Menez, Fenbusquet, and Ménébrat, intersects this dep. from E to W, and divides the waters flowing into the Channel from those which find their way to the gulf of Gascony. Towards its W extremity this chain bifurcates into two branches, one of which, under the name of the mountains of Arrez, enters the dep. of Finistère; the other, under the name of the Black mountains, running SW, likewise enters the same dep. The coast-line is sinuous, and presents numerous bays and small headlands. The rivers which flow into the Channel are the Rance, the Arguenon, the Gouessant, the Goriet, the Trieux, the COT Jandy, and the Guer. Those which flow S are the Meu and the Oust, affluents of the Vilaine; the Lie, an affluent of the Oust, and the Blavet. The Aven and the Hière intersect the W part. Two canals are in course of construction; that of Ille-et-Rance, which will unite those rivers, and that of Blavet and the Aulne making part of the great projected line of communication between Nantes and Brest .- The climate is excessively humid and very variable. The dominant winds are from the N and NW.—Of the superficies, 411,379 hectares are returned as arable ground; 129,635 h. consist of waste land; 54,516 h. are meadows; 40,539 h. are under wood; gardens and orchards occupy 5,532 h.—Agriculture is in a comparatively rude state in this dep. More attention appears to be given to the rearing of cattle, than to any other branch of rural industry. The live stock in 1839 consisted of 89,938 horses, 265,264 cattle, 191,199 sheep, 101,219 pigs, and 6,585 goats. Granite, iron, and salt, are the chief mineral productions. The produce of iron in 1839 was 69,977 quintals, valued at 79,074 francs; of salt 2,999 quintals, valued at 82,764 francs.—Fishing forms the principal branch of industry in the arronds. of Lannion, Saint-Brieux, and Dinan; the manufacture of thread, linen cloth, and leather, forms the leading branch in the arronds. of Guingamp and Loudéac. The dep. contains 1,822 wind-mills, 20 forges and iron-furnaces, and 460 manufacturing establishments of different kinds. The principal articles of export are corn, cattle, horses, cyder, linen, wax, honey, tallow, and provisions. The tonnage of the 18 ports in this dep., in 1841, amounted to 614 vessels = 21,108 tons; and the entire movement of these ports in that year amounted to 732 vessels = 36,764 tons to and from foreign ports; and 14,563 vessels = 186,416 tons in the coastingtrade. — The administrative division of this dep. is into 5 arrondissements, viz., Saint-Brieux, Dinan, Guingamp, Lannion, and Loudéac; which are subdivided into 48 cantons, and 375 communes. It forms the diocese of Saint-Brieux under the archbishopric of Tours.—The pop. in 1801 was 504,303; in 1831, 598,872; in 1841, 607,572; and in 1846, 628,520. In 1834 the number of electors of its 6 deputies to the legislature was 1,517. In 1842 it possessed 5 educational establishments of the second class with 377 pupils; and in 1840, 5 superior communal schools with 306 pupils; and 396 elementary schools, attended by 24,758 students in winter, and 21,363 in summer. The direct taxation in this dep. amounted in 1839 to 2,208,573 francs.
COTESWOLDS. See Cotswold.
COTGRAVE, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 5½ m. ESE of Nottingham, on the Grantham canal. Area 3,350 acres. Pop. 850.
COTHAM, a parish of Nottingham, 4 m. S of Newark, on the E bank of the Devon. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 87.
COTHELSTONE, a parish of Somerset, 61 m. NNW of Taunton. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 104.
COTHERIDGE, a parish of Worcestershire, 4 m. W of Worcester. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 228.
COTHERSTON, a township in the p. of Romald- Kirk, N. R. of Yorkshire, 33 m. NW of Barnard- Castle, on the Tees. Area 8,120 acres. Pop. 566.

COTHUL-E-MULLU, a singular mountain-pass in the Persian prov. of Khorasan, between Dalaki and Cazerun. It begins about 1 m. from Dalaki, and leads up the side of a steep mountain amidst gigantic masses of rock, and over inclined planes of dangerous smoothness, and finally ends in a sharp ascent up the breast of one of the highest masses. No soil covers these rocks, and the only vegetation consists of a few leafless wild almonds. Before clearing the mountain-ridge in this quarter, the road ascends another but less difficult pass called the Cothule-Kumaridge.
COTHY. See CARMARTHENSHIRE.
COTIAS, an island in the bay of Angra-dos-Reis, in the prov. of Rio-de-Janeiro, and district of Parati.

COTIGNAC, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Var, arrond. of Brignoles. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,876; in 1841, 10,579.—The town is situated at the base of a rocky eminence, 11 m. NNE of Brignoles. Pop. in 1841, 3,551. It is noted for its comfits and dried fruit; and has extensive manufactories of silk and leather. Wine, silk, and figs, form its chief articles of trade. Fairs are held 8 times a-year.
COTIGNOLA, a town of the Papal states, in the legation and 38 m. SSE of Ferrara, and 14 m. W of Ravenna, near the l. bank of the Senio. Pop. 2,000.
COTIJUBA, an island of Brazil, in the estuary of the Tocantins, near Belem.
COTINGA, a river of Guayana, descending from the Pacaraima mountains, and flowing SSE to the Takatu, a head-stream of the Parima.
COTILLAS, a town of Spain, in La Mancha, in the prov. of Albacete, partido and 23 m. S of Alcarez, on the NW side of the Sierra Segura.

COTINDIBA, or COTINGUIBA, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Sergipe, which takes its rise in the Serra-Itabaiana, runs in a general direction ESE, and after a total course of 96 m. falls into the Atlantic, 15 m. NE of Sergipe, and near the ruins of the ancient city of Sao-Christovao. It is of considerable breadth, but is only accessible to large vessels at high tide, and by these is not navigable a greater distance than 18 m. Its chief affluent is the Poxim.

COTIOTE, or COTEOTE, a district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malabar, comprising an area-generally mountainous—of 312 sq. m., extending E of Tellicherry to the Western Ghauts. The valleys are fertile, and adapted for the cultivation of rice, cotton, coffee, and sugar. The mountains are covered with wood, but the timber is of little value. This district suffered much from the incursions of Hyder Ali, and of his son Tippu. In 1800 the number of houses was estimated at 4,087.
COTLANDSTOWN, a parish, partly in counties Dublin and Kildare, on the Liffey, 3 m. NE of Kilcullenbridge. Pop. 459.
COTLEIGH, a parish of Devon, 3 m. ENE of Honiton. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 269.
COTNESS, a township in the p. and 5 m. SE of Howden, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 240 acres. Pop. 83.

COTOCLIN CREEK, a river of Frederick co., in the state of Maryland, U. S., which flows through a fine valley, formed by a mountain-range of the same name on the E, and South mountain on the W, and which, after a course of 24 m., falls into the Potomac, 51 m. NW of Washington.

COTON, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 3 m. NW of Cambridge. Area 1,130 acres. Pop. 307.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Market-Bosworth, Leicestershire. Pop. 67.—Also a hamlet in the p. of Ravensthorpe, Northamptonshire. Area 860 acres. Pop. 128.— Also a township in the p. of Hanbury, Staffordshire, 5 m. ESE of Uttoxeter, on the S bank of the Dove. Area 770 acres. Pop. 72.—Also a hamlet in the chapelry of Wigginton, in the same co. Pop. 216.
COTON-IN-THE-ELMES, a township in the p. of Lullington, Derbyshire, 5 m. SW of Burton in Staffordshire. Pop. 351.
COTON-AND-WILLIAMSCOTE, hamlets in the chapelry of Wardington, p. of Cropredy, Oxfordshire. Pop. 193.

COTOPAXI, the most noted of all the volcanoes in the E chain of the Equatorial Andes, in about 0° 40' S lat., and 78° 39' W long. It is situated to the SE of the city of Quito at the distance of 41 m.; between the mountain of Ruminavi, the summit of which, rugged with small separate rocks, extends itself like a wall of enormous height, and the Quelendanna which rises above the line of perpetual snow. The two chains of the Andes are here separated by a long valley, the bottom of which is 9,800 ft. above the level of the sea; so that, rising from this basis, the mountains of C. and Chimborazo do not appear higher than some of the elevated summits of the Alps. C. is the most beautiful of the colossal summits of the high Andes. Its form is a perfect cone, which, being always covered with an enormous layer of snow, shines with the most dazzling splendour at the setting sun, and detaches itself in the most picturesque manner from the azure vault of heaven. This covering of snow conceals from the eye of the observer even the smallest inequalities of the ground; no point of rock, no stony mass, penetrates this coating of ice, or breaks the exact regularity of the conical figure. The summit resembles the sugar-loaf cone which terminates the peak of Teyde; but its height is six times greater than that of the great volcano of the island of Teneriffe. The crater is surrounded by a small circular wall, which when examined with a good telescope looks like a parapet. Near its brink, ledges of rock are to be seen, that are covered with snow, and look at a distance like stripes of the darkest hue. The great steepness of this part of the cone, and the heated air issuing in currents through the deep crevices which surround the cone, and melting the snow, are considered to be the causes of this phenomenon. From these crevices also are thrown out, at the moment of eruptions, scoriæ, pumice-stone, water, and blocks of ice, which are borne down with destructive rapidity to the Rio-Noto, and the other streams issuing from the mountain. C. is also the loftiest volcano at present in activity in the world, being 18,878 ft. above the Pacific, or 2,625 ft. higher than Vesuvius would be were it placed on the top of the Peak of Teneriffe.—Herrera mentions an eruption of this mountain in 1533. After being quiescent for more than two centuries, it burst forth in 1742, and continued its ravages for a long time. Condamine and Bouguer passed nine or ten days upon it, in March 1738, while endeavouring to constitute it one of the points of their geodesic operations; but they were obliged to quit their hazardous station, at the base of the permanent snow, from the impossibility of retaining their Indians with them during the 大 festival of Easter. M. Goden and Don George Juan encamped on it a little lower down for the same purpose. La Condamine returned alone in April to make a last effort; but met with the most untoward accidents. He attempted to scale the peak, over nearly 3,000 ft. of eternal snows, in the month of May; but he could not obtain assistance, the cacique of Mulahala, the Indians, and the Mestizoes of the neighbourhood, all resolutely refusing to listen to his proposals. In 1742, however, he witnessed from a distance the awful spectacle of that eruption which took place after the mountain had been at rest ever since the arrival of the Spaniards in Peru. The snows which had been amassed on the vast cone for more than two centuries suddenly melted on the evening of June 15th, 1742, and the most terrible effects followed. The mountain was in incessant action, particularly on the 27th of Sept. 1743, and on the night of the 30th of Nov. 1744. During these tremendous visitations, cataracts of fire poured out from new mouths in its flanks; cascades of halfmelted snow precipitated themselves on the plains; a sea of boiling water covered in a few minutes many leagues of the surrounding country, and rolled along on its dreadful waves vast inflamed masses, blocks of ice, and fragments of rock. In 1742 the noise of the eruption of C. was heard as far N as La Plata in Colombia, or 300 m. distance as the bird flies; whilst on the S it was heard at Guayaquil, and even at Piura, a distance of 360 m. from the crater. In 1744 the river swelled so prodigiously that the v. of Napo, 60 m. distant in a straight line from the volcano, was swept entirely away between midnight and 1 o'clock in the morning, within about 6 hours after the explosion. The ashes were carried as far as the Pacific, or more than 240 m.; and covered the country, for miles around the mountain, so thickly as to destroy vegetation, being 3 or 4 inches deep even at the distance of 16 m. The shower of ashes was preceded by one of fine earth, of a white, red, and green colour, and of a highly disagreeable odour; and this was itself ushered in by a fall of small gravel, accompanied, it is alleged, in several places by an immense cloud of large white winged insects, with which the earth was instantly covered, but they disappeared before day. In 1738 the peak of C. was measured geometrically, and found to be 3,000 ft. at least higher than the lower line of congelation of eternal snows; the crater was judged also to be about 4,800 ft. in diam. From this vast mouth the fire darted in one grand and awful column to the sky, to the astonishing alt. of nearly 3,000 ft. above the edges of the crater. It was also found that rocks had been shot from the crater to the distance of 9 or 10 m., some of them as large as the cottages of the Indians. In 1756 C. gave dreadful threatenings; and in 1768 its eruptions were most terrific, and such a quantity of ashes were shot out that they completely obscured the sun's light for some hours in the neighbouring town of Hambato. In 1803 the action of the subterranean fire became so powerful that the immense quantity of ice and snow which covered its cone to a vast depth was wholly melted in one night, and at sunrise the cone presented a dark brown appearance; and at Guayaquil, 150 m. to the W, the thunders of the mountain were distinctly heard. Humboldt attempted in 1802 to reach the crater of C., but found it impossible; and he is of opinion, after a near examination of the summit, that the deep ravines by which the cone is surrounded, must ever render the attempt to ascend it altogether fruitless.-From the E side of C. the river Napo takes its rise, and from the S the Cotucha and Alagues, which afterwards unite and flow into the Maranon; on the N rises the Rio-del-Pedregal, an affluent of the Esmeraldas, which empties itself | of Buntingford. Area 1,760 acres. Pop. 465. COT into the Pacific. The above cut represents the summit of this mountain.

COTRONE, a maritime town of Naples, cap. of the district of the same name, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, 34 m. ENE of Catanzaro, at the foot of Monte-Carvaro, and at the embouchure of the Esaro in the Ionian sea. Pop. 4,530. It is strongly fortified with a castle and batteries, which front the sea, and are separated from the town by a ditch. It has a cathedral, 5 other churches, 2 convents, 2 hospitals, and a custom-house; but the streets are in general narrow, and the houses mean and ill-built. The advantages of its situation render its commerce-consisting chiefly in cattle, grain, wine, oil, turpentine, and silk, the produce of the locality - extremely active. A fair is held once a-year. The low clayey hills which surround the town produce fine wheat; and the vale of the Neto is renowned for its pastures. This town—the ancient Crotona—was the birthplace of the wrestler Milo, and was noted for the athletic power of its inhabitants.—The district of C. comprises the 6 cant. of Ciro, Cotrone, Policastro, Santa-Severina, Strongoli, and Umbriatico.
COTRONEI, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Calabria-Ultra, district and 20 m. WNW of Cotrone, cant. and 8 m. W of Santa-Severina, on a hill between the Neto and Facina. Pop. 1,378.

COTSWOLD, or COTTESWOLD HILLS, a tract of undulating country which extends across the E part of Gloucestershire, from near Chipping-Camden on the N, to within a short distance of Bath and Cirencester. Their highest points are Broadway-beacon, alt. 1,086 ft.; and Cleeve-hill, alt. 1,134 ft. The greater part of the district is so much elevated above sea-level as to delay the harvest about a fortnight beyond the period in the surrounding low grounds. The general appearance of the tract is that of a series of level but elevated plains, falling at intervals into gentle valleys, through which the natural drainage is carried from the W and steepest side by short courses to the Severn; and from the E side into the upper branches of the Thames. The general features of the country present a pleasing succession of villages and homesteads. The farms are large. The C. breed of sheep is the principal description of live stock. See GLOUCESTERSHIRE.

COTTACOOMACASA, a village in Upper Guinea, in the Gold coast territory, 15 m. N of Abra. The environs are pleasing. In their picturesque little hills of fanciful outline, and encircled with small groves of many varieties of tint, they reminded Bowdich "of the celebrated ride by Grongar-hill from Carmarthen to Llandilo."
COTTACOTTA, a town of Hindostan, in the Balaghaut territories, 56 m. N of Cuddapah.

COTTAM, a hamlet in the p. of Preston, Lancashire. Pop. 53.—Also a chapelry in the p. of South Leverton, Nottinghamshire. Area 900 acres. Pop. 89.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Langtoft, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 41.
COTTAPATAN, a town of Hindostan, in the Carnatic, 52 m. S of Tanjore.
COTTBUS. See Kottbus.
COTTEE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bahar, 90 m. S by W of Patna.

COTTENHAM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 63 m. N of Cambridge. Area 7,510 acres. Pop. 1,833. The district is celebrated for its cheese, the produce of dairies supplied from the rich pasture-land along the Ouse. The v. suffered severely from fire in the month of April 1850. Archbishop Tennison, and the Lord-chancellor Cottenham of the present day. were natives of this parish.
COTTERED, a parish of Hertfordshire, 21 m. W *
COTTERSTOCK, a parish of Northamptonshire, 2 m. NNE of Oundle. Area 690 acres. Pop. 204.
COTTESBATCH, a parish of Leicestershire, 11/4 m. SSW of Lutterworth. Area 1,230 acres. Pop. 82.
COTTESBROOK, a parish of Northamptonshire, 9 m. NNW of Northampton. Area 3,860 acres. Pop. 252.
COTTESFORD, a parish of Oxfordshire, 51 m. N of Bicester. Area 1,520 acres. Pop. 187.
COTTESMORE, a parish of Rutlandshire, 4 m. NNE of Oakham. Area 2,420 acres. Pop. 670.
COTTINGHAM, a parish of Northamptonshire, 2 m. SW of Rockingham. Area 3,220 acres. Pop. 1,033.—Also a p. in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 4½ m. NW of Kingston-on-Hull. Area 8,530 acres. Pop. 2,618.
COTTINGWITH (EAST), a chapelry in the p. of Aughton, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 308.

COTTON, a township in the p. of Sandbach in Cheshire, 3 m. E by N of Middlewich. Area 350 acres. Pop. 101.-Also a township in the p. of Wem, in Salop, 7½ m. E of Wem. Pop. 504.—Also a township in the p. of Alveton, in Stafford, 5 m. NE of Cheadle. Pop. 519.—Also a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. WNW of Mendlesham. Area 1,820 acres. Pop. 545.—Also a township in the p. of Hovingham, N. R. of Yorkshire, 71 m. SSE of Helmsley. Area 930 acres. Pop. 158.
COTTON, a township in Switzerland co., in the state of Iowa, U.S. Pop. 1,450.
COTTON-GIN-PORT, a village in Monroe co., in the state of Missouri, U.S., 180 m. NE of Jackson, at the head of the navigation of the Tombigbee.
COTTON-GROVE, a village in Davidson co., in the state of N Carolina, 102 m. W of Raleigh.—Also a village in Madison co., in Tennessee, 137 m. WSW of Nashville.
COTTON-PLANT, a village in Tippah co., in Missouri, U. S., 202 m. NNE of Jackson.
COTTON-RIVER, a village in Henry co., in Georgia, U. S., 72 m. W by N of Milledgeville.
COTTONVILLE, a village in Marshall co., in Alabama, U. S., 109 m. NE of Tuscaloosa.
COTTRELVILLE, a village in St. Clair co., in Michigan, U.S. Pop. 602.
COTUIT, a village in Barnstable co., in Massachusetts, 68 m. SSE of Boston.
COTUMBAH, a town of Hindostan, in the presidency of Bengal, prov. and 95 m. WSW of Bahar, near the l. bank of the Great Pompon river.
COTUY (EL), a town in the island of Hayti, in the dep. of Nord, 40 m. NNE of Saint-Domingo, near the r. bank of the Yuna, about 18 m. from its mouth. In its vicinity are copper and iron mines.

COUARA, a village of Central Africa, near the river Koraba, which is said to fall into the Joliba in the neighbourhood of Sego, and which, where Caillie crossed it, in February, was 10 ft. deep, and from 50 to 60 fath. wide. A considerable quantity of cotton and millet are grown in the environs of this v.
COUARDE (LA), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, isle of Ré, 12 m. W of La Rochelle. Pop. 1,947.

COUBCABIA, a village of Central Africa, in Darfur, 41 m. W of Cobbé. It is a considerable town, and forms the key of the commercial roads leading to the westward from Cobbé, and the depot of the merchandise brought from that quarter. A market is held here twice a-week, in which the chief medium of exchange for articles of small value is salt, and which is celebrated for its tokeas, or strong coarse cotton cloths, and leather sacks. The inhabitants are Fulahs, Arabs, and Berguese. COU Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Brie-Comte-Robert. Pop. 512.
COUBISOU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Estaing. Pop. 2,911.
COUBLANC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Chauffailles. Pop. 1,242.
COUBLEVIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Voiron. Pop. 1,517.
COUBON, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Loire, cant. of Le Puy. Pop. 2,181.
COUCHES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Autun.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. 12,616. -The town is 12 m. ESE of Autun. Pop. in 1836, 3,050.
COUCKELAERE, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, arrond. and 9 m. SW of Bruges. Pop. 3,916.
COUCLIA, a village in the island of Cyprus, 1 m. NNE of Mandria, supposed by some to occupy the site of the ancient Cythera; by others, that of Arsinoe. There is an ancient palace here.
COUCOULI, a village of Turkey, in Albania, in the valley of Zagori, near the l. bank of the Voioussa or Aous.
COUCOURON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardèche, arrond. of Argentière.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. 5,386.— The town is 30 m. W of Privas. Pop. 1,045.
COUCY-LE-CHATEAU, a canton, commune. and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Laon.—The cant. comprises 34 com. Pop. 18,145. -The town, 12 m. WSW of Laon, has a pop. of 839.
COUDEKERQUE-BRANCHE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. and 3 m. SE of Dunkerque. Pop. 1,402.
COUDES-MONTPEYROUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Issoire. Pop. 1,473.
COUDEVILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Brehal, 10 m. S of Coutance. Pop. 1,002.

COUDIAC (LE PETIT), or PETICODIAC, a river of New Brunswick, rising near the sources of the Kenebecasis, and flowing into Shepody bay in the bay of Fundy, after a winding but rapid course of 40 m. to the NE, and then 20 m. to the S. Ships occasionally proceed up this river as far as Dorchester for timber. The flood-tide in this river is accompanied by a bore or tidal wave, which at spring-tides is 5 or 6 ft. high. At the Bend, or point where the river turns S, common tides rise 22 ft. 8 in., high tides 28 ft. 8 in.; while at Dorchester island common tides rise 36 ft., high tides 42 ft.
COUDRAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Ecouis. Pop. 402.
COUDRAY-LA-NEUVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Oise, cant. of Noailles. Pop. 332.

COUDRAY-SAINT-GERMER (LE), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Beauvais .- The cant., comprising 18 com., had a pop. of 11,055 in 1841.—The v., 9 m. W of Beauvais, had a pop. of 501.

COUDRE (ISLE), or COUDRES, an island in the St. Lawrence, close to the N shore and in front of St. Paul's bay. It is 6 m. long, and 21 m. broad. Its soil is fertile, and it supports one of the oldest settlements in Canada. It is composed of greywacke and slate rocks. Pop. 300.
COUDRECEAU, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Thiron-Gardais. Pop. 779. Pottery ware is extensively made here.
COUDRECIEUX, a commune of France, in the
COUBERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of | dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Bouloire, 9 m. NW of Saint-Calais. Pop. 1,422. It has an extensive glassmanufactory.
COUDURES, a town of France, in the dep. of the Landes, cant. and 6 m. SSE of Saint-Sever, between the Bas and Gabas. Pop. 834.

COUERON, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Etienne-de-Mont-Luc, on the r. bank of the Loire, 24 m. SE of Savenay. Pop. in 1841, 4,214. It has a good careening port, and an active trade in linen, wine, brandy, &c. Kaolin is wrought in the environs.
COUESMES, a commune of France, in the dep. and 13 m. NNW of Mayenne, cant. of Arbrieres. Pop. 1,600.

COUESNON, a river of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, which takes its rise to the E of Fougères; flows E to Saint-Christophe-de-Valains; thence bends N; passes Antrain; runs along the confines of the dep. of the Manche; and falls into the bay of Cancale a little below Pontorson, after a total course of 66 m., of which 12 m. only-from Antrain downwards-are navigable at high water.
COUFFE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Ligné, 7 m. WNW of Ancenis. Pop. 1,910.
COUFFOULEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Tarn, cant. of Rabastens. Pop 1,178.
COUFFY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Saint Aignan. Pop. 626. Gun-flints are wrought here.
COUFLENS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Oust. Pop. 1,267.
COUGHALL, or CAUGHALL, a township in the p. of Backford, Cheshire, 3 m. N of Chester. Area 340 acres. Pop. 16.
COUGHTON, a parish of Warwickshire, 2 m. NNW of Alcester, on the E bank of the Arrow. Area 3,070 acres. Pop. 955.

COUHE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Vienne, arrond. of Civray. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,916; in 1841, 11,930. The town is 19 m. from Civray, on the r. bank of the Dive. Pop. in 1841, 1,913. It has manufactures of coarse cloth, and an active trade in chestnuts, cray-fish, mules, &c.
COUILLET, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 5 m. E of Marchienne-au-Pont, on the line of the railway from Braine-le-Comte to Namur. Pop. 883. There are extensive iron-works here.
COUILLY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Crecy. Pop. 721. It has several flour-mills.

COUIZA, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Aude, arrond. of Limoux. The cant. comprises 24 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,608; in 1841, 8,829. The village is 8 m. S of Limoux, on the road from Paris to Montlouis, and at the confluence of the Sals with the Aude, the latter of which is here crossed by a handsome bridge. Pop. in 1846, 958. It has a spinning-mill; and in the environs is a bed of gypsum. A fair is held here once a-year.
COUKELAIRE, or Coclaar, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of W Flanders, 6 m. WNW of Thorout.

COULAN, or Quilon, a maritime town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Travancore, 90 m. NNW of Cape Comorin, and 76 m. SSE of Cochin, in N lat. 8° 53', E long. 76° 39', on the Indian ocean, at the mouth of a navigable river. The inhabitants, according to Herbert 100,000 in number, are chiefly Hindus. It contains an ancient temple, and 3 Catholic churches, and on the coast towards Sadras are seven richly sculptured temples cut out of the solid rock. It has a considerable trade in cotton, pepper, ginger, cardamons, &c. The environs afford rice, bananas, pine-apples, and other fruit. The adjacent sea | Pop. 1,034. COU abounds with excellent fish, tortoises, &c. This town was formerly of considerable importance. Its foundation is dated as early as A. D. 825. It was taken from the Portuguese by the Dutch in 1662, and subsequently fell into the hands of the English.

COULANGE-LA-VINEUSE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Auxerre. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,630; in 1841, 8,958. The town lies in a fertile locality, 8 m. S of Auxerre. Pop. in 1841, 1,328. The environs afford good wine.

COULANGE-SUR-YONNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Auxerre. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,549; in 1841, 7,872. The town is on the l. bank of the Yonne, 20 m. S of Auxerre. Pop. in 1846, 1,247. It has a considerable trade in wine, timber, agricultural implements, &c.; and has three annual fairs.
COULANS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Loire, near the l. bank of the Geax, 11 m. W of Le Mans. Pop. 1,891. It has a paper-mill.

COULAURES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Savignac, on the l. bank of the Loire, a little above the confluence of that river with the Isle, and 18 m. NE of Périgueux. Pop. 1,419.
COULDEVIE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. and 11 m. SE of Voiron, and 14 m. NW of Grenoble. Pop. 1,429.

COULEUVRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Allier, cant. of Lurcy-le-Sauvage, 25 m. WNW of Moulins. Pop. 1,479. It has an extensive bottle manufactory. Gypsum abounds in the locality.

COULFO, a town of Central Africa, the most considerable market-town in Nyffé, 4 m. from Tabria, on the river May-varrow. It is surrounded by a high wall, outside of which is a deep and broad ditch; and contains between 15,000 and 16,000 inhabitants. Markets are daily held here, to which traders resort in vast numbers from Bornu and Soccasu to the NE, and from the sea-coast to the W. Slaves, horses, asses, and horned cattle, silk, cotton, natron, beads, turbans, scarlet caps, goora nuts, malaguetta pepper, and red wood, are among the articles of merchandise exposed for sale here.—The country for miles round C. is studded with walled towns, open villages, and clusters of houses. It is 2 days' journey from Yaouri. COULIBŒUF, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, arrond. of Falaise. The cant. comprises 27 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,605; in 1841, 9,865. The town is 7 m. NE of Falaise, on the r. bank of the Dives. Pop. 442.
COULIMER, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Pervenchères, 5 m. SW of Mortagne-sur-Huine.
COULL, a parish of Aberdeenshire, at the head of Strathcromar. Area 12 sq. m. Pop. 744.
COULLONS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. and 10 m. SW of Gien. Pop. 1,937.
COULMIER-LE-SEE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côte-d'Or, cant. and 10 m. SSW of Châtillon-sur-Seine. Pop. 540. It has an iron-work.
COULOISY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Attichy. Pop. 181.
COULOMB (POINT), a headland on the NW coast of Australia, in Slat. 17° 21', 17 m. N of Cape Boileau.
COULOMB (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Cancale. Pop. 2,209.—Also a commune in the dep. of the Lot-et- Garonne, cant. of Lauzun. Pop. 1,125.
COULOMBIERS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Beaumont-sur-Sarthe.

COULOMMIERS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seineet-Marne. The arrond., comprising an area of 91,770 hectares, contains 4 cant., viz.: Coulommiers, La Ferte- Gaucher, Rebais, and Rozoy-en-Brie. Pop. in 1831, 53,363; in 1846, 54,323. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,809; in 1841, 16,001. The town is situated on the r. bank of the Grand-Morin, 15 m. SE of Meaux, and 30 m. NE of Melun. Pop. in 1789, 6,834; in 1821, 3,467; in 1831, 3,335; and in 1841, 3,658. With the exception of the castle— now in ruins—built by the Duchess of Longueville, and an hospital, this town contains no buildings worthy of notice. It has, however, an active trade in grain, flour, melons, cheese, cattle, pigs, sheep, wool, fodder, and leather. Fairs are held here twice a-year, and markets weekly.

COULON, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, cant. and 6 m. W of Niort, on the r. bank of the Sevre-Niortaise. Pop. 1,480.—Also a village in the dep. of the Cher, cant. of Graçay, 28 m. W of Bourges. Pop. 1,117.
COULONCES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. and 3 m. NNW of Vire. Pop. 1,145.
COULONCHE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Messey. Pop. 1,805. It has some manufactures of linen and ticking.
COULONGES, a town of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Fere-en-Tardenois, 17 m. NE of Chateau-Thierry, on the r. bank of the Ovion. Pop. 423.
COULONGES-SUR-LAUTIZE, or Coulonges-

LES-ROYAUX, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux-Sevres, arrond. of Niort. The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,428; in 1841, 14,165. The town occupies a pleasant situation, 15 m. NNE of Niort. Pop. in 1846, 1,997. It is the entrepot of the timber and staves from Gatine, the wines of Saintonge, and of wool for the manufactures of Gatine; and has extensive tanneries and manufactories of swanskin, floor-cloths, &c. Fairs are held twelve times a-year.

COULOUBRIER, a river of France, in the dep. of the Var, which takes its rise in the hill of the same name, to the SW of Revest, and after a course of about 11 m. unites with the Argens, 12 m. S of Muy. It dries up in summer.
COULOUCHE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Saint-Gervais-de-Messey, 11 m. ENE of Domfront. Pop. 1,670. It has a manufactory of linen.
COULOUVRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of St. Poix, 14 m. NW of Mortain. Pop. 1,275.
COULSDON, a parish of Surrey, 5 m. SSW of Croydon, near the Croydon line of railway. Area 3,930 acres. Pop. 1,041.
COULSTON (East), a parish of Wilts, 54 m. ENE of Westbury. Area 1,080 acres. Pop. 105.
COULSTON (WEST), a tything of the p. of Edington, Surrey. Pop. 144.

COULTER (LOCH), a lake in the p. of St. Ninians, Stirlingshire. It is 2 m. in circumf., and discharges itself into the Bannockburn. It is said that during the great earthquake in 1755, by which Lisbon was destroyed, this loch was greatly agitated.
COULTON, a parish in Lancashire, 5 m. NNE of Ulverstone. Area 13,330 acres. Pop. 1,983.

COULY-DROOG, or Cooly-Droog, a town and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Mysore, district and 10 m. SE of Bedenore, and 150 m. NW of Seringapatam, on the Gorgket. The town contains about 200 houses. The fort stands on the summit of an adjacent mountain, and is regularly built.
COUMASSIE. See COOMASSIE. . COU

COUNCIL BLUFFS, a military station on the W bank of the Missouri, in the territory of Iowa, U. S., in N lat. 41° 45', W long. 96°; at an elevation of 800 ft. above sea-level. The mean annual temp. here, calculated on 5 years' observations, is 51° 08'.
COUND, a parish in Salop, 5½ m. NW of Much- Wenlock. Area 5,890 acres. Pop. 809.
COUNDON, a township in the p. of St. Andrew- Auckland, in Durham, 2 m. ESE of Bishop-Auckland. Pop. 990.—Also a hamlet, 1 m. from Coventry, in the co. of Warwick. Area 880 acres. Pop. 181.

COUNIGNAN, a village in Central Africa, in the kingdom of Bambarra, 10 m. NE of Medina. It is situated in a plain covered with gravel, on which nothing is to be seen but bushes at considerable distances from each other, but which is cultivated in the rainy season.
COUNTESTHORPE, a chapelry and village in the p. of Blaby, in Leicestershire, 6 m. S of Leicester. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 815.
COUNTISBURY, a parish in Devon, 15 m. E by N of Ilfordcombe. Area 3,290 acres. Pop. 815.

COUNTY-LONE, a village in Niagara co., in the state of New York, U. S., 272 m. W by N of Albany. -Also a v. in Davies co., in N Carolina, 146 m. W of Raleigh.—Also a v. in Campbell co., in Georgia, 117 m. WNW of Milledgeville, -Also a v. in Chambers co., Alabama, 151 m. E of Tuscaloosa.—Also a v. in Scott co., Missouri, 60 m. E of Jackson.
